prompt: 2026 market language for AI-AGENT SECURITY, TRUST, and ATTESTATION. How do practitioners, founders, CISOs, and AI-vendor sellers actually TALK on X and the web in 2026 about: getting an AI agent through an enterprise security review / procurement; agent identity and provenance; verifiable evidence vs questionnaires; 'agent trust', 'agent passport', 'agent attestation', 'AI BOM', 'signed evidence'. Quote real recent posts/phrases (@handle + date) and real product copy (URLs). Which words/frames RESONATE and convert, which are overused cliches to avoid, and what vocabulary is emerging that a signed-agent-evidence product should adopt to sound native in 2026 without sounding like hype. Conclude with a short glossary of the 8-12 highest-signal terms kolm should use.
content:
**In 2026, enterprise discussions on AI-agent security, trust, and attestation center on moving beyond self-reported questionnaires and marketing claims to cryptographic, hardware-rooted, auditable proofs that enable procurement, identity governance, and runtime controls.** CISOs, founders, vendors, and practitioners emphasize verifiable provenance, pre-execution checks, and continuous monitoring to close the "governance gap" between rapid agent deployment and enterprise risk tolerance. Key pain points include shared/static credentials, unverifiable security claims (e.g., "83% of vendors' security claims cannot be independently verified"), identity models built for humans rather than autonomous agents, and the need for signed artifacts tied to standards like OWASP LLM Top 10, NIST AI RMF, and MITRE ATLAS.[[1]](https://x.com/Basemail_ai/status/2062709196433555590)[[2]](https://www.opaque.co/resources/articles/six-agencies-five-governments-one-message-your-ai-agents-cant-ignore)

**Procurement/security reviews** have shifted from feature checklists to demands for demonstrable assurance. Agents stall in pilots without "verifiable governance" or "cryptographic attestation" proving they run expected/unmodified code, authenticate with fresh proofs before privileged actions, and produce comprehensive artifacts.[[2]](https://www.opaque.co/resources/articles/six-agencies-five-governments-one-message-your-ai-agents-cant-ignore)[[3]](https://www.uipath.com/blog/ai/ciso-guide-securing-agentic-ai) Surveys and reports highlight that while 91% of orgs deploy agents, only ~10% have mature management strategies; identity governance (unique verifiable identities, short-lived/task-scoped credentials, behavioral verification) is the critical blocker.[[4]](https://www.strata.io/blog/agentic-identity/the-ai-agent-identity-crisis-new-research-reveals-a-governance-gap/)[[5]](https://cloudsecurityalliance.org/artifacts/securing-autonomous-ai-agents)

**Agent identity and provenance** focus on replacing "trust me bro" with machine-verifiable mechanisms: Ed25519 challenge-response, SPIFFE/SVID-style short-lived identities, W3C DIDs, or on-chain attestations. Provenance extends to models, data, inference execution, and decision traces. Hardware attestation (rooted in NVIDIA/Intel/AMD silicon or TEEs) and signed receipts are recurring requirements.[[6]](https://christian-schneider.net/blog/non-human-identity-governance-gap-ai-agents/)[[7]](https://x.com/NekiWeb3/status/2063580933807030532)

**Verifiable evidence vs. questionnaires** is a major theme—enterprises reject static claims in favor of dynamic, signed, independently attestable evidence (e.g., "Attested Evidence Pack — a hardware-signed cryptographic receipt"). This includes pre-production testing against public standards plus runtime monitoring.[[2]](https://www.opaque.co/resources/articles/six-agencies-five-governments-one-message-your-ai-agents-cant-ignore)[[8]](https://newsroom.workday.com/2026-06-02-Workday-Launches-Agent-Passport-to-Test,-Verify,-and-Continuously-Monitor-Every-AI-Agent-in-the-Enterprise)

**Specific terminology in use (real recent examples):**
- **"Agent Passport"**: Workday launched "Agent Passport" (June 2, 2026) to "test, verify, and continuously monitor every AI agent" (built or third-party) with results "tied to a public industry standard" (OWASP LLM Top 10, NIST AI RMF, MITRE ATLAS) for a "signed, auditable record" and independent attestation (e.g., Cisco AI Defense partner). It addresses prompt injection, data leaks, etc., before production.[[8]](https://newsroom.workday.com/2026-06-02-Workday-Launches-Agent-Passport-to-Test,-Verify,-and-Continuously-Monitor-Every-AI-Agent-in-the-Enterprise)[[9]](https://www.prnewswire.com/news-releases/workday-launches-agent-passport-to-test-verify-and-continuously-monitor-every-ai-agent-in-the-enterprise-302787979.html) Similar concepts appear in open-source/IETF drafts (e.g., Agent Passport System/APS with Ed25519 passports and signed documents) and products like APort (agent passport + control plane with "pre-action authorization" and "proof signed").[[10]](https://aport.io/)[[11]](https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/draft-pidlisnyi-aps/01/)
- **Attestation language**: "Require agents to perform cryptographic attestation where agents must prove they are running expected and unmodified code"; "Authenticate agents with fresh cryptographic proofs"; "hardware attestation"; "runtime integrity"; "Attested Evidence Pack."[[2]](https://www.opaque.co/resources/articles/six-agencies-five-governments-one-message-your-ai-agents-cant-ignore)[[12]](https://www.linkedin.com/pulse/ai-attestation-proving-agent-can-trusted-before-acts-raghavaiah-avula-icacc)
- **Evidence/provenance**: "Signed evidence," "verifiable execution," "provenance" (data/model/inference), "cryptographic receipt," "JWS-signed ACT/HALT receipts."[[2]](https://www.opaque.co/resources/articles/six-agencies-five-governments-one-message-your-ai-agents-cant-ignore)[[7]](https://x.com/NekiWeb3/status/2063580933807030532)[[13]](https://x.com/abdul15100/status/1994303623963025715)
- **X/Twitter examples** (recent as of mid-2026): @Basemail_ai (Jun 5, 2026) on a security "report card" noting "83% of vendors' security claims cannot be independently verified" and the gap between agent capabilities and provable authorization.[[1]](https://x.com/Basemail_ai/status/2062709196433555590) @NekiWeb3 (Jun 7, 2026) on hardware attestation + model identity for "cryptographic proof of where [responses] came from."[[7]](https://x.com/NekiWeb3/status/2063580933807030532) Earlier: @CryptoEights (Aug 19, 2025) on "AI Agent Trust" frameworks with community-driven evaluation and documentation for decision-making.[[14]](https://x.com/CryptoEights/status/1957629260660896202)
- **AI BOM/AIBOM**: Established as an "AI Bill of Materials"—a machine-readable inventory of models, datasets, dependencies, prompts, tools, identities, and configurations for supply-chain transparency, governance, and security (extensions of SBOMs). Used for discovery of agents/models/APIs in infrastructure.[[15]](https://www.paloaltonetworks.com/cyberpedia/what-is-an-ai-bom)[[16]](https://checkmarx.com/learn/ai-cybersecurity/what-is-an-aibom/)[[17]](https://www.armosec.io/blog/ai-bill-of-materials/)

**Resonating frames/words that convert** (native, practical, standards-tied, proof-oriented): cryptographic/hardware attestation, signed/auditable record or evidence/receipt/pack, verifiable (identity/provenance/execution), pre-action authorization or runtime integrity/monitoring, agent identity governance, independent/third-party attestation, tied to public standards (NIST/OWASP/Mitre), short-lived/task-scoped credentials or behavioral verification. These appear in CISO playbooks, government guidance (e.g., joint agency mandates for cryptographic proofs), vendor positioning (Auth0, OPAQUE, Strata, Zenity, Workday), and standards work (IETF, OpenID Foundation, NIST).[[2]](https://www.opaque.co/resources/articles/six-agencies-five-governments-one-message-your-ai-agents-cant-ignore)[[18]](https://openid.net/oidf-responds-to-nist-on-ai-agent-security/)[[6]](https://christian-schneider.net/blog/non-human-identity-governance-gap-ai-agents/) They emphasize auditability, accountability, and closing specific gaps (e.g., "the gap between what agents CAN do and what enterprises can PROVE").

**Overused clichés to avoid**: Vague "trust," "secure by design," or "AI safety" without mechanics; pure self-reported questionnaires or marketing claims; "black box" without proposing verifiable alternatives; generic "governance" or "compliance" detached from cryptographic/hardware proofs or standards mapping. "Trust me bro" is explicitly called out as the current flawed state.[[19]](https://x.com/theonejvo/status/2064173732260934124)

**Emerging vocabulary for a signed-agent-evidence product** (to sound native, non-hype): Focus on "attested evidence," "cryptographic receipts," "verifiable provenance" (across model/data/inference/agent actions), "pre-execution attestation" or "policy-enforced signed proofs," "agentic identity" with faceted/scoped delegation, integration with existing SBOM/AIBOM workflows, and "continuous attested monitoring." Tie explicitly to standards and independent verifiers. Use precise, mechanism-focused language like "JWS-signed," "hardware-rooted," "fresh cryptographic proofs," or "monotonic narrowing" in delegation chains.

**Short glossary of highest-signal terms (8–12) for native 2026 usage**:
- **Agent Passport**: Signed, standards-mapped identity/verification artifact for pre-prod testing + continuous monitoring (e.g., Workday implementation).
- **Cryptographic/hardware attestation**: Proof that code/config/runtime is expected/unmodified (often TEE/silicon-rooted).
- **Attested Evidence Pack / Signed Evidence / Cryptographic Receipt**: Hardware- or key-signed artifact documenting actions, policy, data, tools, and results.
- **Verifiable Provenance**: Traceable, auditable lineage for models, data, inference, and agent decisions/actions.
- **AIBOM / AI-BOM**: Machine-readable inventory of AI components (models, datasets, tools, identities, configs) for discovery/governance/supply-chain security.
- **Pre-action authorization**: Policy check + proof before tool execution or privileged calls.
- **Runtime integrity / Continuous attestation**: Ongoing verification of agent state/behavior post-deployment.
- **Agent identity governance**: Lifecycle management of unique, verifiable, short-lived/scoped non-human identities (vs. shared human creds).
- **Independent/third-party attestation**: Signed results from verified external attestors (not self-reported).
- **Fresh cryptographic proofs**: Per-action or time-bound authentication/authorization evidence.
- **Standards-mapped attestation**: Evidence explicitly tied to OWASP LLM Top 10, NIST AI RMF, MITRE ATLAS, etc.
- **Verifiable execution**: Cryptographic proof of model version, inputs, conditions, and outputs without exposing sensitive data.

This language positions solutions as practical infrastructure for procurement and operations rather than aspirational hype.
